reference_type_and_applicant.title: AP Calculus & Physics Teacher / STEM Club Advisor

reference_evaluation.academic_or_work_performance_achievement:
I have had the pleasure of teaching Daniel Alejandro Morales in both AP Calculus AB (11th grade) and AP Physics 1 (12th grade), as well as advising him for two years in our after-school STEM Club and Math Team at North Ridge High School. Academically, Daniel is among the top 2–3 students I have taught in my 18-year career. He currently holds a 4.0 unweighted GPA and a 4.6 weighted GPA, with a consistent record of A grades in the most rigorous courses our school offers, including AP Calculus AB, AP Physics 1, AP Chemistry, AP Computer Science Principles, and Honors English.

Daniel demonstrates exceptional problem‑solving ability and intellectual curiosity. In Calculus, he was not content to simply learn procedures; he routinely stayed after class to ask about the theoretical underpinnings of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and how the concept of limits extends into real analysis. On multiple occasions, he independently researched topics such as Taylor series and basic differential equations—content beyond the AP curriculum—and then shared his findings with classmates in informal mini‑lessons that were clear and engaging.

In AP Physics 1, Daniel has a rare ability to connect mathematical models to real‑world phenomena. For example, during our rotational motion unit, he designed a small experiment at home using a bike wheel and a smartphone gyroscope app to collect angular acceleration data. He then brought his data to class, graphed it in Desmos, and led a short discussion on sources of error and how frictional torque affected his results. This was entirely self‑initiated and demonstrated a level of initiative and scientific thinking more typical of a strong college sophomore than a high school senior.

Daniel’s written work is meticulous and well‑organized. His lab reports consistently exceed expectations: he clearly states hypotheses, designs thoughtful procedures, carefully records data, and provides thorough analyses that include uncertainty calculations and thoughtful discussions of limitations. On our most challenging cumulative exam in Calculus—one that historically has an average score in the low 70s—Daniel scored a 98%, the highest score in all my sections. He also scored a 5 on the AP Calculus AB and AP Computer Science Principles exams as a junior, and based on his current performance, I fully expect him to earn 5s in AP Physics 1 and AP Chemistry this year.

Beyond raw achievement, what stands out most is Daniel’s growth mindset and intellectual humility. When he encounters a difficult problem, he does not give up or simply ask for the answer. Instead, he breaks the problem into smaller parts, tries multiple approaches, and reflects on what each attempt teaches him. I have seen him remain after school for more than an hour working through a single challenging physics free‑response problem, not because it was graded, but because he genuinely wanted to master the concept. This persistence and intrinsic motivation will serve him extremely well in a demanding college environment.

In short, Daniel is not only high‑achieving, but truly intellectually engaged. He thinks deeply, asks sophisticated questions, and actively seeks feedback. I have no doubt that he will thrive academically in any rigorous STEM program he pursues.
reference_evaluation.attendance_record:
Daniel’s attendance record is excellent. Over the two years I have taught and advised him, he has not missed a single day of my classes for an unexcused reason. According to our school’s attendance portal, he has had only three absences during his entire junior and senior years, all of which were excused due to illness (verified by the school nurse and his parents). He also had two late arrivals this year, both on days when there was unusually heavy snow and multiple bus delays; he emailed me ahead of time to let me know he might be a few minutes late and arrived visibly flustered that he was not on time.

For extracurricular commitments, Daniel is similarly reliable. As the STEM Club Advisor, I track attendance for meetings and weekend events. Daniel has attended approximately 95% of weekly STEM Club meetings over the past two years and has been present at every major competition and outreach event we have organized, including our Saturday middle school robotics workshop and the regional Science Olympiad invitational. On the rare occasions when he cannot attend due to a family obligation or medical appointment, he informs me at least a day in advance and follows up afterward to catch up on any missed planning or preparation.

In group projects, Daniel is the student teammates never have to worry about. For example, during our AP Physics capstone project—a multi‑week assignment requiring regular after‑school work—Daniel was consistently the first to arrive and the last to leave. His group members reported that he often reminded them of upcoming work sessions and deadlines, ensuring that everyone stayed on track. I have never had to chase him down for missing work or unexplained absences.

Overall, Daniel’s attendance and punctuality reflect a high level of maturity, respect for others’ time, and a strong sense of responsibility. I would characterize his attendance as exemplary.
reference_evaluation.character_leadership_and_school_and_or_c:
Daniel is an exceptionally grounded, thoughtful, and empathetic young person whose character is every bit as impressive as his academic record. He is soft‑spoken but confident, and he leads more by example and quiet encouragement than by seeking the spotlight. I have observed him in classroom settings, during club activities, and at community events, and in each of these contexts he consistently demonstrates integrity, kindness, and genuine concern for others.

In terms of leadership, Daniel has served as co‑president of our STEM Club for the past year and as captain of the Math Team. When he first joined STEM Club as a sophomore, he was one of the quieter members, often staying in the background and focusing on his own projects. Over time, he grew into a leadership role, initially by taking responsibility for organizing materials and documenting project plans. Last year, when our previous club president graduated, Daniel stepped up. Rather than simply inheriting the title, he took the initiative to survey club members about what kinds of activities they wanted—more hands‑on projects, guest speakers, and outreach—and then helped design a semester‑long plan that reflected those interests.

One of the clearest examples of Daniel’s leadership occurred during our “Community STEM Day,” an event where high school students host interactive science and engineering stations for local elementary and middle school students. Daniel volunteered to lead the “Build Your Own Solar Car” station. He not only designed the activity and tested it multiple times beforehand, but also created simple, bilingual instruction sheets in English and Spanish so that parents and students who primarily speak Spanish could fully participate. Throughout the event, I watched him kneel down to eye level with younger students, patiently explain basic concepts like energy conversion and friction, and encourage them to troubleshoot when their cars did not move as expected. Several parents approached me afterward to say how grateful they were that someone took the time to explain things in Spanish and made their children feel welcome.

Daniel’s character also shows in smaller, everyday moments. He is the student who stays after a lab to help clean up equipment without being asked, who notices when a quieter classmate is struggling and offers to review the homework with them at lunch, and who thanks me at the end of almost every class. Last spring, when one of his classmates lost a family member, Daniel quietly organized a card and small care package from the STEM Club members. He did not publicize his role in this; I only learned about it because another student mentioned it to me in passing.

Outside of school, Daniel is actively involved in his community. He volunteers twice a month at the Eastwood Community Center, where he helps run a Saturday coding workshop for middle school students using Scratch and basic Python. He has also participated in neighborhood clean‑up days and assisted his church’s food pantry by helping sort and distribute donations. His volunteer supervisor at the community center has emailed me more than once to praise Daniel’s reliability and his ability to connect with younger students who may be intimidated by coding.

Daniel’s bilingual background (he speaks both English and Spanish fluently) has made him an informal bridge between our school and Spanish‑speaking families. During parent‑teacher conference nights, he has, with parental and administrative permission, occasionally helped his peers’ families understand school resources and opportunities, particularly around STEM programs and college preparation. He is always respectful of boundaries and defers to staff, but his presence has clearly helped some families feel more comfortable and included.

In sum, Daniel’s leadership is characterized by humility, service, and a genuine desire to lift others up. He is exactly the kind of student who strengthens the culture of a school and a community, not through loud declarations, but through consistent, thoughtful action.
reference_evaluation.recommendation:
I recommend Daniel Alejandro Morales for this scholarship with the highest possible enthusiasm and absolutely no reservations. In my nearly two decades of teaching advanced mathematics and science, he stands out as one of the most capable, hardworking, and ethically grounded students I have encountered.

Daniel combines intellectual excellence, a relentless work ethic, and a deep commitment to serving others. He has already demonstrated that he can handle college‑level coursework, as shown by his performance in AP Calculus, AP Physics, and other advanced classes, as well as his strong standardized test scores and independent projects. Equally important, he approaches learning with curiosity and humility, always seeking to understand the “why” behind a concept rather than just the “how.”

From a personal standpoint, Daniel is mature, dependable, and emotionally intelligent. He collaborates well with peers from diverse backgrounds, listens carefully to others’ perspectives, and contributes thoughtfully to discussions. His leadership in STEM Club, Math Team, and community outreach initiatives illustrates that he is not only prepared to succeed in a rigorous academic environment, but also to contribute positively to the campus community.

I am confident that the financial support provided by this scholarship would not only help Daniel access opportunities that might otherwise be out of reach for him as a first‑generation college student, but would also be an excellent investment in a young person who will give back many times over. He has expressed a strong interest in pursuing mechanical or electrical engineering with a focus on sustainable energy solutions, and based on everything I have seen, I fully believe he has the talent, perseverance, and character to achieve these goals.

Without hesitation, I give Daniel my strongest recommendation and would rank him in the top 1–2% of students I have worked with. Should you require any additional information, I would be more than happy to speak further about his qualifications and character.
reference_evaluation.other_remarks_comments_not_addressed_abo:
One aspect of Daniel’s background that may not be immediately evident from his transcript is the extent to which he has balanced his academic commitments with significant family responsibilities. Daniel is the eldest of three children in a household where both parents work long hours—his mother as a home health aide and his father as a warehouse forklift operator. As a result, Daniel often helps care for his younger siblings after school, assists with homework, and translates important documents and conversations from English to Spanish for his parents.

Despite these responsibilities, Daniel has managed to maintain an outstanding academic record and deep involvement in extracurricular activities. There have been times when he has come to my classroom early in the morning to work on problem sets because he knew that his after‑school hours would be dedicated to watching his siblings while his parents were at work. Rather than using his circumstances as an excuse, he has quietly and consistently found ways to make it all work. This level of time management, resilience, and self‑discipline is unusual in a high school student and speaks volumes about his character.

Another point worth noting is Daniel’s growth in confidence over the past three years. When I first met him, he was hesitant to speak up in class, even when he clearly understood the material. Through encouragement and his own efforts, he has gradually become more comfortable sharing his ideas and asking questions in front of his peers. This year, he volunteered to present his group’s engineering design project at our district‑wide STEM Showcase, standing in front of a large audience that included school board members, parents, and local engineers. His presentation was clear, well‑structured, and delivered with a calm assurance that would have been hard to imagine when he was a sophomore.

Finally, I want to emphasize that Daniel is not motivated solely by grades or external recognition. In a reflective essay he wrote for my class, he described a moment when a middle school student at the community center told him, “I didn’t think kids like me could do coding.” Daniel wrote that this comment stayed with him and solidified his desire to pursue engineering not just as a career, but as a way to create opportunities for students from underrepresented backgrounds. He sees education and technology as tools for empowerment, and he has already begun to live out that belief in small but meaningful ways.

I believe that with the additional support and validation that a scholarship like this provides, Daniel will be able to attend a four‑year university without needing to work excessive hours to cover tuition, allowing him to fully engage in research, internships, and campus organizations. I am confident he will make the most of every opportunity afforded to him and will continue to represent his family, his community, and your scholarship program with integrity and distinction.

evaluator_information_and_signature.title_position_affiliation_to_student: AP Calculus & AP Physics Teacher; STEM Club Advisor, North Ridge High School
